title: Hierarchical clustering on asymmetric networks
speaker: Facundo Mémoli
abstract: The problem of determining clusters in a data set admits different interpretations depending on whether the data is metric, symmetric but not necessarily metric, or asymmetric. Whereas there is a good degree of understanding of what are the natural methods for clustering symmetric data, the landscape of methods for clustering asymmetric data is not so well understood. It is possible to study and characterize hierarchical clustering methods that operate on asymmetric networks in an axiomatic manner.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Asymmetric networks and dendrogram targets
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines finite asymmetric dissimilarity networks, records the two-node network δ₂(α,β) used later for normalization, and defines dendrograms as monotone scale-indexed partitions that start at singletons and eventually merge to one block.

proof_stages.label: Basic asymmetric hierarchical-clustering constructions
proof_stages.summary: Several candidate methods are constructed: max-symmetrization followed by single linkage gives the upper method H^U, loop reachability gives the lower method H^L, and bounded-length loop relations give intermediate H^k with H^2=H^U and H^∞=H^L.

proof_stages.label: Functorial formulation of clustering methods
proof_stages.summary: Networks and dendrograms are made into categories using weight-nonincreasing maps and partition-compatible maps, and a clustering method is required to be a functor expressing that decreasing weights should not make clustering harder.

proof_stages.label: Extremal bracketing theorem
proof_stages.summary: Under the two-node normalization that δ₂(α,β) merges at max(α,β), every functorial clustering method lies between the lower and upper constructions, and the same theorem implies uniqueness of the normalized method on symmetric networks.

proof_stages.label: Additional axioms: scale preservation, excision, and complexity
proof_stages.summary: To narrow the class of functorial methods, the talk introduces scale preservation, linear scale preservation, excisiveness as local consistency on dendrogram blocks, and a motivating notion of descriptive complexity.

proof_stages.label: Representability by motif-based symmetrization
proof_stages.summary: Representable methods are defined as single linkage after a symmetrization functor λ determined by a family of generalized network motifs through expansion constants and infima over motif maps hitting the two points being compared.

proof_stages.label: Characterization of representable methods
proof_stages.summary: The main characterization states that, among normalized functorial clustering methods, representability is equivalent to excisiveness plus linear scale invariance, leaving the classification of non-excisive and nonrepresentable methods as an open direction.

examples.name: Average and complete linkage as familiar symmetric methods
examples.note: Average and complete linkage are cited, alongside single linkage, as standard hierarchical-clustering rules on symmetric networks to motivate symmetrizing asymmetric data; no new idea is derived from them here.
examples.engagement_reason: average and complete linkage are only named as familiar symmetric clustering choices, while only single linkage is defined and used

examples.name: Linear change of units in all dissimilarities
examples.note: Scaling all distances up is used to make the scale-preservation axiom concrete, since the dendrogram parameter must be reparametrized back to compare the same clustering structure.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines how a scaling change acts on networks and dendrograms and traces the resulting effect on clustering scale

examples.name: Two-point unit motif recovering max symmetrization
examples.note: The two-point motif with both directed weights equal to one is mapped into a two-point asymmetric network to show that the motif expansion formula returns max(alpha,beta), hence recovers the earlier max symmetrization.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the motif construction on the two-point unit motif and computes that the required scaling is max(alpha,beta), recovering max symmetrization

passes.reason: the two-point bidirectional unit motif is a minimal symmetric template whose induced symmetrization is the pointwise maximum
